question_text: Computerized Accounting System (CAS) refers to:
explanation: A Computerized Accounting System uses computers and accounting software to record, classify, summarise, and report financial transactions. It replaces manual books of accounts with electronic records.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Using calculators to add up totals
Using computers and software to maintain accounting records
Scanning paper records into digital format

elimination_hints.hint: Calculators assist computation but don't maintain complete accounting records.
Scanning digitises paper; CAS processes and manages accounting data electronically.
Spreadsheets are one tool; CAS is a complete system for all accounting functions.

question_text: Which of the following is an advantage of Computerized Accounting over Manual Accounting?
explanation: Computerized Accounting offers speed and accuracy as a key advantage. Computers process thousands of transactions in seconds with minimal errors, unlike manual accounting which is slow and error-prone.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Speed and accuracy in processing transactions
No need for any accounting knowledge

elimination_hints.hint: Accounting knowledge is still essential — software is a tool, not a replacement for knowledge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking software eliminates need for expertise.

elimination_hints.hint: Auditing is still required to verify the accuracy and compliance of records.
Computers require electricity, hardware, and software to function.

question_text: Which of the following is a LIMITATION of Computerized Accounting?
explanation: CAS requires significant initial investment in hardware, software, training, and setup. This high cost of implementation is a major limitation, especially for small businesses. Other limitations include dependence on electricity, risk of data loss, and need for trained staff.
subject: accountancy

options.text: It is very slow in processing data
High initial cost of hardware, software, and training
It produces less accurate results than manual accounting

elimination_hints.hint: Speed is an ADVANTAGE of CAS, not a limitation.
Generating reports instantly is a key advantage of CAS.
CAS is MORE accurate than manual — fewer arithmetic errors.

question_text: Statement: In a Computerized Accounting System, the basic accounting principles (Double Entry, Matching, Going Concern) remain the same.
explanation: This is TRUE. CAS only changes the METHOD of recording — from manual to electronic. The underlying accounting principles, concepts, and conventions remain exactly the same. Double entry, accrual, matching principle, etc. all apply.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: CAS changes the tool, not the rules. Double entry and other principles still apply.

question_text: The components of a Computerized Accounting System include:
explanation: A CAS comprises: (1) Hardware (computers, printers), (2) Software (accounting programs like Tally), (3) People (trained operators), (4) Procedures (rules for data entry and processing), and (5) Data (financial transactions).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Hardware, software, people, procedures, and data

elimination_hints.hint: People (operators) and procedures are also essential components.
Hardware and people are also needed — you can't run software without computers and operators.
Internet is optional. People, procedures, and data are core components.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including internet but excluding key components.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of Computerized Accounting?
explanation: Dependence on electricity and hardware is a DISADVANTAGE, not an advantage. Power failure, hardware breakdown, or software crashes can disrupt the entire accounting process. Advantages include speed, accuracy, automatic reports, and easy backup.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Easy storage and retrieval of data

elimination_hints.hint: Automatic calculation IS an advantage — reduces manual effort and errors.
Easy storage IS an advantage — no bulky paper files needed.
Instant reports IS an advantage — P&L, B/S available with a click.

question_text: In the context of CAS, 'Codification of Accounts' means:
explanation: Codification of accounts means assigning unique numeric or alphanumeric codes to each account (e.g., 1001 for Cash, 2001 for Sales). This helps in quick identification, sorting, and processing of accounts by the computer system.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Assigning unique codes to each account for computer processing
Writing accounts in a secret language
Classifying accounts into assets and liabilities only

elimination_hints.hint: Codification uses standard codes, not secret languages.
Classification is broader. Codification specifically assigns codes for identification.
Encryption is a security measure. Codification is for identification and processing.

question_text: The process of recording transactions using accounting software instead of manual books is called ______ accounting.
explanation: Computerized accounting (or automated accounting) is the process of recording, classifying, and summarising financial transactions using computer software instead of traditional manual books like journals and ledgers.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Manual accounting is the traditional method using books — the opposite of what's described.
Statistical accounting relates to data analysis, not software-based recording.
Forensic accounting is investigation of financial fraud.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Computerized Accounting reduces the chances of errors compared to manual accounting.
Reason (R): Computers perform arithmetic operations with 100% accuracy and can apply validation checks on data entry.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Computers do not make arithmetic mistakes (unlike humans), and software can validate entries (e.g., checking that debits equal credits, verifying account codes). This significantly reduces errors.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains the mechanism — accuracy and validation checks reduce errors.
R is true — computers DO perform arithmetic with 100% accuracy.
A is correct — CAS does reduce errors compared to manual systems.

question_text: In manual accounting, Trial Balance must be manually prepared. In CAS:
explanation: In CAS, the Trial Balance is automatically generated by the software at any time. The computer continuously maintains the ledger balances and can instantly produce a Trial Balance. This saves time and effort.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Trial Balance is not needed at all
Trial Balance is automatically generated by the software
Trial Balance must still be manually prepared
Trial Balance is replaced by Cash Flow Statement

elimination_hints.hint: Trial Balance is still needed for verification — but it's auto-generated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking CAS eliminates need for TB.

elimination_hints.hint: The whole point of CAS is automation — TB is generated automatically.
TB and Cash Flow are different statements — one doesn't replace the other.

question_text: The concept of 'Grouping of Accounts' in CAS means:
explanation: Grouping of accounts means organising individual accounts into logical groups and sub-groups (e.g., Current Assets → Cash, Bank, Debtors; Fixed Assets → Land, Building, Machinery). This hierarchical structure helps in report generation and analysis.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Creating one account for all transactions
Keeping accounts in alphabetical order only
Organising accounts into logical groups and sub-groups for reporting
Deleting unused accounts from the system

elimination_hints.hint: One account for all would violate basic accounting principles.
Alphabetical order is sorting, not grouping by nature/function.
Deleting accounts is maintenance, not grouping.

question_text: Which of the following features distinguishes CAS from a simple spreadsheet-based system?
explanation: CAS provides built-in double-entry validation (automatic debit-credit matching), predefined accounting structures, automated report generation, and audit trails. A simple spreadsheet lacks these built-in accounting safeguards.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Built-in double-entry validation, automated posting, and audit trail

elimination_hints.hint: Both spreadsheets and CAS can store numbers — not a distinguishing feature.
elimination_hints.misconception: Identifying a common feature as unique.

elimination_hints.hint: Spreadsheets are actually better at charts. CAS specialises in accounting logic.
Both can print. CAS's advantage is accounting-specific features.
elimination_hints.misconception: Identifying a common feature as unique.

question_text: Statement: Data backup is an important feature of Computerized Accounting to protect against data loss.
explanation: This is TRUE. Data backup is critical in CAS. Regular backups protect against data loss due to hardware failure, software crashes, power outages, or cyber attacks. Most accounting software provides built-in backup functionality.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Without backup, a single hardware failure can destroy years of accounting data.

question_text: Which of the following accounting software is most commonly used in India?
explanation: Tally (now Tally Prime) is the most widely used accounting software in India. It is used by millions of businesses for maintaining books of accounts, GST compliance, inventory management, and financial reporting.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Photoshop is image editing software, not accounting software.
AutoCAD is engineering/design software.
PowerPoint is a presentation tool, not accounting software.

question_text: The sequence of data processing in a Computerized Accounting System is:
explanation: The correct sequence is: (1) Source documents (invoices, receipts) → (2) Data entry (input through vouchers) → (3) Processing (automatic posting, calculation) → (4) Output (reports — TB, P&L, B/S). This follows the Input → Process → Output model.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Source Documents → Data Entry (Input) → Processing → Output (Reports)

elimination_hints.hint: This is the reverse. Processing always follows input, and output comes last.
Processing cannot happen before input — you need data first.
Reports come at the end, not the beginning. Vouchers → Journal → Ledger → Reports.

question_text: In CAS, a 'Voucher' is:
explanation: In CAS (especially Tally), a voucher is a form/screen used for entering accounting transactions. Different voucher types include: Payment Voucher, Receipt Voucher, Journal Voucher, Sales Voucher, Purchase Voucher, Contra Voucher, etc.
subject: accountancy

options.text: A data entry form/screen for recording transactions
A backup file of accounting data

elimination_hints.hint: Discount coupons are commercial offers, not accounting vouchers.
Reports are outputs. Vouchers are input forms for data entry.
Backup files store data copies. Vouchers are transaction entry forms.

question_text: Which of the following voucher types is used for cash withdrawals from bank in Tally?
explanation: Contra Voucher is used for transactions between cash and bank accounts — cash deposits into bank, cash withdrawals from bank, and fund transfers between banks. It does not affect income or expenses.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Payment voucher is for paying expenses/liabilities. Cash withdrawal from bank is a contra entry.
Receipt voucher is for receiving income. Bank withdrawal is cash-to-bank, not income.
Journal voucher is for non-cash adjustments (depreciation, provisions). Cash-bank is contra.

question_text: An 'Audit Trail' in CAS refers to:
explanation: An audit trail is a chronological record of all changes made to accounting data — who made the change, when, and what was changed. It provides accountability and helps detect unauthorized modifications. This is a key advantage of CAS over manual systems.
subject: accountancy

options.text: A record of all changes made to data — who, when, and what was changed
A trail of papers leading to the auditor's office
A backup copy of all financial reports
A list of all auditors who have reviewed the accounts

elimination_hints.hint: Audit trail is a digital concept — a log of data changes, not physical papers.
Backup is a copy of data. Audit trail is a log of changes/modifications.
It tracks data changes, not auditor names.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing audit trail with auditor records.

question_text: MIS Reports in the context of CAS stands for:
explanation: MIS stands for Management Information System. MIS Reports are generated by CAS to provide management with structured information for decision-making — such as outstanding receivables, payment schedules, budgets vs actuals, and performance analysis.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: MIS is a computerized system, not manual.
While MIS may include income data, this is not what MIS stands for.
MIS is about information for management decisions, not investment schedules.

question_text:
Match the CAS concepts:

Column I:
(i) Contra Voucher
(ii) Audit Trail
(iii) Codification
(iv) MIS Reports

Column II:
(P) Assigning unique codes to accounts
(Q) Cash-to-Bank or Bank-to-Cash entry
(R) Reports for management decision-making
(S) Log of all data modifications
explanation: Contra Voucher → Cash-Bank transactions (Q). Audit Trail → Log of data modifications (S). Codification → Unique codes for accounts (P). MIS Reports → Management decision-making reports (R).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Contra Voucher is Cash-Bank (Q), not codification (P).
Contra Voucher is Cash-Bank (Q), not audit trail (S).
Audit Trail is log of modifications (S), not codification (P).
